headline: Meryl Streep Slams Hollywood's Character Reduction: 'Complexity Is Dying'
lede: Oscar legend Meryl Streep delivers a scorching critique of modern storytelling, targeting how contemporary cinema flattens complex characters into simplistic archetypes.
body:
In a blistering indictment of current cinematic trends, Meryl Streep is challenging Hollywood's reductive approach to character development. The four-time Academy Award winner argues that the industry's increasing tendency to transform nuanced personalities into marketable tropes represents a fundamental artistic failure.

Speaking candidly about her upcoming "The Devil Wears Prada" sequel, Streep highlighted how her iconic Miranda Priestly character defies simplistic categorization. Her character represents a masterclass in narrative complexity — simultaneously demanding and compassionate, intimidating yet deeply human.

The veteran actress suggests that streaming platforms and franchise-driven storytelling have accelerated this trend toward character minimalism. By prioritizing quick narrative beats and recognizable archetypes, filmmakers sacrifice the rich, messy humanity that makes storytelling compelling.

Industry insiders have long recognized Streep's commitment to character depth. Her performances consistently challenge audiences by presenting multidimensional personalities that resist easy emotional shorthand. From Sophie's tortured choice to Miranda Priestly's layered professionalism, Streep has repeatedly demonstrated how truly great acting transcends stereotypical limitations.
key_facts: Upcoming 'Devil Wears Prada' sequel features complex protagonist
Four-time Oscar winner champions narrative complexity
